text.title: Power/Interest grid and difficult stakeholder types
text.summary: The power/interest grid determines communication frequency and channel per quadrant; stakeholder type (silent, dominant, negative, absent, changing) requires a specific facilitation response.

rule.statement: Map each stakeholder to a quadrant: High Power + High Interest = Manage Closely (weekly 1-on-1s, involve in decisions); High Power + Low Interest = Keep Satisfied (bi-weekly summary, escalate issues); Low Power + High Interest = Keep Informed (monthly status); Low Power + Low Interest = Monitor (available on request).
rule.rationale: Under-communicating with high-power stakeholders creates surprises and vetoes. Over-communicating with low-interest stakeholders wastes their time and reduces your credibility.

rule.statement: Do not assign quadrant positions from job titles alone. Political influence often does not track seniority — seek organizational context from a human before placing anyone.
rule.rationale: An informal influencer with a mid-level title placed in "Monitor" who then vetoes the project is the most expensive stakeholder management failure.

rule.statement: Identify the stakeholder type before the session and select the facilitation response that matches. Do not use the same approach for all stakeholder types.
rule.rationale: Each type represents a different underlying dynamic. A "Silent" stakeholder needs direct questions and written async input; using the same open-group format produces the same silence.

list.item: Silent (won't share): direct questions addressed by name, written async input before the meeting
Dominant (talks over others): use brainwriting or round robin to enforce equal turn-taking
Negative (shoots down ideas): "Yes, and..." facilitation rule; Reverse Brainstorm to channel criticism productively
Absent (misses meetings): async input requests, written summary with confirmation required
Changing (shifts requirements): require written confirmation per requirement; maintain a dated change log

antipattern.description: Accepting verbal agreement from a "Changing" stakeholder without a written confirmation.
antipattern.reason: Verbal agreements are repudiated under project pressure. A written confirmation email creates a dated record that prevents requirement drift from becoming a dispute.

text.reference: Requirement capture card with validation status
